verses.flow: James, a bondservant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ, to the twelve tribes in the Dispersion: Greetings.
verses.note: GREEK: "Doulos" (δοῦλος, Strong's G1401) means "bondservant" or "slave"—James identifies himself not by familial relation to Jesus but by servitude, a remarkable self-designation for the Lord's own brother. HISTORICAL: "The twelve tribes in the Dispersion" (diaspora, G1290) addresses Jewish believers scattered beyond Palestine, indicating an early date and Jewish-Christian audience. The greeting "chairein" (χαίρειν) is the standard Greek epistolary salutation, echoing the Jerusalem Council letter James presided over (Acts 15:23).

verses.flow: Count it all joy, my brothers, when you fall into various trials,
verses.note: GREEK: "Peirasmos" (πειρασμός, G3986) can mean either "trial" (external testing) or "temptation" (internal enticement); here the context indicates outward trials. "Poikilos" (ποικίλος, G4164) means "many-colored, variegated, diverse"—trials come in manifold forms. THEOLOGICAL: Joy is not a feeling about suffering but a deliberate reckoning ("hēgēsasthe," aorist imperative) based on knowledge of what trials produce.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: knowing that the testing of your faith produces endurance.
verses.note: GREEK: "Dokimion" (δοκίμιον, G1383) refers to the "means of testing" or "proving," used of assaying metals for purity. Compare 1 Pet 1:7 where the same word describes faith tested by fire. "Hypomonē" (ὑπομονή, G5281) is not passive patience but active, steadfast endurance under pressure.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And let endurance have its perfect work, so that you may be perfect and complete, lacking in nothing.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine
verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: But if any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask from God, who gives to all generously and without reproach, and it shall be given to him.
verses.note: GREEK: "Haplōs" (ἁπλῶς, G574) means "generously, simply, without reservation"—it is a hapax legomenon in the NT. "Oneidizo" (ὀνειδίζω, G3679) means "to reproach, upbraid"—God gives without shaming the asker. THEOLOGICAL: God's character as the generous giver stands in contrast to double-minded human asking (vv. 6-8), establishing the theological foundation for prayer throughout the epistle.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present passive participle dative singular masculine
verb present passive participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: But let him ask in faith, doubting nothing; for the one who doubts is like a wave of the sea, driven by the wind and tossed about.
For let not that man suppose that he shall receive anything from the Lord—
a double-minded man, unstable in all his ways.
verses.note: GREEK: "Dipsychos" (δίψυχος, G1374) literally "double-souled," is a word found only in James in the NT (here and 4:8) and nowhere in prior Greek literature, making it likely a Jamesian coinage. "Akatastatos" (ἀκατάστατος, G182) means "unstable, restless"—the double-minded man is characterized by fundamental instability in every path he walks.

verses.flow: But let the lowly brother boast in his exaltation,
and the rich in his humiliation, because as a flower of the grass he shall pass away.
For the sun rose with its scorching heat and dried up the grass, and its flower fell off, and the beauty of its appearance perished; so also the rich man in his pursuits shall wither away.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: Blessed is the man who endures trial, because having become approved he shall receive the crown of life, which He promised to those who love Him.
verses.note: GREEK: "Stephanos tēs zōēs" (στέφανος τῆς ζωῆς)—the "crown of life" is a genitive of apposition: the crown which is life itself. "Stephanos" (G4735) refers to the victor's wreath, not the royal diadem (diadēma). TYPOLOGY: The crown of life promised to those who endure trials echoes Rev 2:10 and reflects the eschatological reward motif found throughout Second Temple Jewish literature. THEOLOGICAL: The promise is specifically to "those who love Him"—endurance is framed as an expression of covenant love for God.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Let no one being tempted say, "I am tempted by God," for God is not temptable by evil, and He Himself tempts no one.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: James makes a critical distinction between "peirasmos" as trial (v. 2) and "peirazo" as temptation to evil. God tests but never tempts. "Apeirastos" (ἀπείραστος, G551) is another rare term, meaning either "unable to be tempted" or "inexperienced in evil"—both senses reinforce God's absolute separation from moral evil. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This passage implicitly rejects any theology that attributes moral evil to the divine realm; evil originates in creaturely desire, not in the heavenly council.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But each one is tempted, being drawn away and enticed by his own desire.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine
verb aorist passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: Then desire, having conceived, gives birth to sin; and sin, having been brought to completion, brings forth death.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: James presents a birth-and-death metaphor for the progression of sin: desire conceives (syllabousa, a term used for pregnancy), gives birth (tiktei) to sin, and sin when fully grown brings forth (apokyei) death. This echoes the Genesis 3 pattern where desire led to transgression and transgression to death (Gen 2:17; Rom 5:12). TYPOLOGY: The sequence inverts the life-giving pattern of v. 18, where God's will "brings forth" (apokyeō, same verb) life through the word of truth.

verses.flow: Do not be led astray, my beloved brothers.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: Every good giving and every perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights, with whom there is no variation or shadow cast by turning.
verses.note: GREEK: "Dosis" (δόσις, G1394) emphasizes the act of giving; "dōrēma" (δώρημα, G1434) emphasizes the gift itself—James uses both to express the fullness of divine generosity. "Parallagē" (παραλλαγή, G3883) is a hapax legomenon meaning "variation" or "change," and "aposkiasma" (ἀποσκίασμα, G644) is also a hapax meaning "shadow cast by turning"—both are astronomical terms. DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Father of lights" (patēr tōn phōtōn) designates God as Creator of the celestial luminaries (Gen 1:14-18), and unlike the heavenly bodies which shift and cast shadows, He is utterly unchanging.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Having willed it, He brought us forth by the word of truth, that we should be a kind of firstfruits of His creatures.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Boulētheis" (βουληθείς)—"having willed," emphasizes the sovereign deliberation of God in bringing forth believers. "Apokyeō" (ἀποκυέω, G616) "brought forth" uses birth language, contrasting with v. 15 where desire "brings forth" death. TYPOLOGY: "Firstfruits" (aparchē, G536) draws on the OT offering of the first portion of harvest to God (Lev 23:10); believers are the inaugural portion of a renewed creation, anticipating the full harvest of new creation (Rom 8:19-23).

verses.flow: Know this, my beloved brothers: but let every man be swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath;
for the wrath of man does not produce the righteousness of God.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore, having put away all filthiness and abundance of wickedness, in meekness receive the implanted word, which is able to save your souls.
verses.note: GREEK: "Emphytos" (ἔμφυτος, G1721) means "implanted" or "innate"—the word that has been engrafted into the believer, not merely heard externally. "Rhyparia" (ῥυπαρία, G4507) is a hapax legomenon meaning "moral filth." THEOLOGICAL: The implanted word is declared "able to save your souls" (psychas)—James affirms the saving power of the gospel word while simultaneously demanding that reception of it be accompanied by moral transformation.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But become doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving yourselves.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: Because if anyone is a hearer of the word and not a doer, this one is like a man beholding the face of his birth in a mirror;
for he beheld himself and has gone away and immediately forgot what manner of man he was.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But the one who looked intently into the perfect law, that of liberty, and continued in it, having become not a forgetful hearer but a doer of the work—this one shall be blessed in his doing.
verses.note: GREEK: "Parakyptō" (παρακύπτω, G3879) means "to stoop down and look intently into," suggesting careful, sustained examination—the same verb used of Peter and John peering into the empty tomb (John 20:5, 11). "Nomon teleion ton tēs eleutherias"—"the perfect law, that of liberty"—identifies Torah as fulfilled and perfected in Christ, a law that liberates rather than enslaves (Gal 5:1). THEOLOGICAL: The one who perseveres in this law becomes not merely a hearer but a "doer of the work," and the blessing is located in the doing itself.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: If anyone considers himself to be religious, yet not bridling his tongue but deceiving his own heart, this one's religion is vain.
Religion pure and undefiled before God and the Father is this: to look after orphans and widows in their affliction, and to keep oneself unstained from the world.
verses.note: GREEK: "Thrēskeia" (θρησκεία, G2356) refers to the outward expression of worship and religious observance—James redefines it in ethical terms. "Aspilon" (ἄσπιλον, G784) means "without spot, unstained." THEOLOGICAL: Pure religion before God is defined not by ritual but by two actions: care for the most vulnerable (orphans and widows, echoing Deut 10:18; Ps 68:5; Isa 1:17) and personal holiness in separation from worldly corruption. This verse programmatically introduces the social ethics that dominate chapters 2-5.

verses.flow: My brothers, do not with partiality hold the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory.
verses.note: GREEK: "Partiality" (prosōpolēmpsíais, cf. Strong's G4382) is a compound from prosōpon ("face") and lambánō ("to receive")—literally "face-receiving," i.e., judging by outward appearance. This is a rare noun form found almost exclusively in biblical and early Christian literature. THEOLOGICAL: James links partiality directly to a violation of authentic faith in Christ, whom he calls "our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory" (tēs doxēs)—a genitive of quality or apposition identifying Christ Himself as the Glory, echoing the divine kavod/shekinah presence of God (cf. 1 Cor 2:8; Ex 40:34).

verses.flow: For if there should come into your assembly a man with a gold ring, in bright clothing, and there should come in also a poor man in filthy clothing,

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: and you look upon the one wearing the bright clothing and say, "You sit here in a good place," and to the poor man you say, "You stand there, or sit below my footstool,"
have you not made distinctions among yourselves and become judges with evil reasonings?
verses.note: GREEK: "Made distinctions" (diekríthēte, G1252) can mean "to be divided" or "to waver," suggesting internal division and double-mindedness. "Evil reasonings" (dialogismōn ponērōn) recalls Jesus' warnings about what proceeds from the heart (Mark 7:21). THEOLOGICAL: Partiality reveals corrupted judgment; believers have set themselves up as judges operating by worldly, demonic criteria rather than by the wisdom from above (Jas 3:15-17).

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: Listen, my beloved brothers: did not God choose the poor of the world to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom which He promised to those who love Him?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: God's election of the poor inverts worldly values. "Rich in faith" and "heirs of the kingdom" echo Jesus' Beatitudes (Matt 5:3; Luke 6:20). The kingdom is promised specifically to those who love God, echoing the Shema tradition (Deut 6:5) and Paul's language in 1 Cor 2:9 and Rom 8:28. TYPOLOGY: The poor as God's chosen inheritors recalls Israel's own identity as a small, enslaved people whom God chose—not for their power but by His sovereign grace (Deut 7:7-8).

verses.flow: But you have dishonored the poor man. Do not the rich oppress you, and do they not themselves drag you into courts of judgment?

words.m: verb aorist passive participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: Do they not themselves blaspheme the good name which has been called upon you?
If indeed you fulfill the royal law according to the Scripture, "You shall love your neighbor as yourself," you do well.
verses.note: GREEK: "Royal law" (nómon basilikón, G937) may mean the law belonging to the King (Christ) or the law that governs all other laws—the supreme law. TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Lev 19:18 (LXX), which Christ Himself identified as the second greatest commandment (Matt 22:39). James thus identifies the Torah's love command as the reigning principle of the new covenant community under King Jesus.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: But if you show partiality, you work sin, being convicted by the law as transgressors.
For whoever shall keep the whole law, yet stumble in one point, has become guilty of all.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: James articulates the unity and indivisibility of God's law. To break one commandment is to violate the whole, because the law reflects the singular will of one Lawgiver (v.11). This does not teach salvation by perfect law-keeping but rather exposes the impossibility of partial obedience as a basis for self-righteousness. The logic drives the hearer toward dependence on mercy (v.13).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For He who said, "Do not commit adultery," also said, "Do not murder." Now if you do not commit adultery but you do murder, you have become a transgressor of the law.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: So speak and so act as those who are about to be judged through the law of liberty.

words.m: verb aorist active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: For judgment is without mercy to the one who has shown no mercy. Mercy triumphs over judgment.
verses.note: GREEK: "Without mercy" (anéleos, G448) is a rare adjective—a hapax legomenon in the NT. "Triumphs over" (katakaucháomai, G2620) means to boast against or exult over, depicting mercy as victorious over judgment in an almost personified contest. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is the theological hinge of the chapter: God's mercy, shown through merciful living, overrides condemnation. It connects backward to the partiality discussion and forward to the faith-and-works argument, grounding both in the character of God Himself.

verses.flow: What is the profit, my brothers, if someone says he has faith but does not have works? Is the faith able to save him?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This begins the famous faith-and-works discourse (vv.14-26). James does not contradict Paul (Rom 3:28) but addresses a different problem: a dead, merely verbal confession devoid of obedient action. The rhetorical question expects a negative answer in Greek (mē dýnatai)—"that faith cannot save him, can it?" The article hē pístis ("the faith") points to this particular kind of faith—the empty, workless kind just described.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: If a brother or sister should be naked and lacking daily food,
and one of you should say to them, "Go in peace, be warmed and be filled," but you do not give to them the things needful for the body, what is the profit?
So also faith, if it does not have works, is dead by itself.
But someone will say, "You have faith, and I have works." Show me your faith apart from the works, and I will show you my faith from my works.
You believe that God is one. You do well; even the demons believe—and shudder.
verses.note: GREEK: "Shudder" (phríssousin, G5425) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, describing a visceral, horrified trembling. THEOLOGICAL: The Shema confession "God is one" (Deut 6:4) is affirmed as correct belief, yet James devastatingly notes that demons share this orthodox theology. Mere intellectual assent to true doctrine, even the most foundational confession of Israel, does not constitute saving faith. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The demons (daimónia) are portrayed as spiritual beings who possess accurate theological knowledge of God's oneness yet remain in rebellious terror before Him—knowledge without allegiance.

verses.flow: But do you wish to know, O empty man, that faith apart from works is barren?

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Was not Abraham our father justified by works, having offered up Isaac his son upon the altar?
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The binding of Isaac (the Aqedah, Gen 22:1-19) is the supreme OT test of faith-in-action. Abraham's willingness to offer his son demonstrated the reality of the faith credited to him in Gen 15:6. James reads Genesis narratively: the declaration of righteousness in Gen 15 was vindicated and brought to completion by the obedient act in Gen 22. THEOLOGICAL: "Justified by works" (ex érgōn edikaióthē) here means "shown to be righteous" or "vindicated"—the demonstrative, evidentiary sense of dikaióō, complementing Paul's forensic-declarative usage (Rom 4:2-3).

verses.flow: You see that faith was working together with his works, and by the works the faith was made complete.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And the Scripture was fulfilled which says, "And Abraham believed God, and it was reckoned to him as righteousness," and he was called a friend of God.
verses.note: GREEK: "Was fulfilled" (eplērōthē, G4137) indicates that the Scripture reached its fullest expression—Gen 15:6 was not merely a past event but an ongoing reality brought to completion through Abraham's obedient life. "Friend of God" (phílos theoû) draws on 2 Chr 20:7 and Isa 41:8 (LXX), a unique covenantal title. TYPOLOGY: Abraham's faith-and-works unity foreshadows the believer's union with Christ, in whom faith is always active through love (Gal 5:6).

verses.flow: You see that by works a man is justified, and not by faith alone.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular feminine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And likewise was not also Rahab the harlot justified by works, having received the messengers and sent them out by another way?
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Rahab the harlot (Josh 2:1-21; 6:22-25) was a Canaanite woman—a Gentile outsider of the lowest social standing—whose faith was demonstrated by the concrete act of receiving Israel's spies and sending them out safely. TYPOLOGY: James deliberately pairs Abraham (the patriarch of Israel, male, righteous) with Rahab (a Gentile, female, harlot) to show that justifying faith expressed in works transcends every social and ethnic boundary. Both appear in Christ's genealogy (Matt 1:2, 5). GREEK: "Messengers" (aggélous, G32) here refers to human messengers/spies, the basic meaning of the word before its specialized use for heavenly beings.

verses.flow: For just as the body apart from the spirit is dead, so also faith apart from works is dead.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The body-spirit analogy is the conclusive summary. Just as a body without spirit (pneúmatos) is a corpse, faith without works is spiritually dead—not merely deficient but lifeless. This is not a works-salvation argument but an insistence that genuine, living faith necessarily produces obedient action, as breath animates the body. STRUCTURE: This verse forms an inclusio with v.17, bracketing the entire argument: faith without works is dead (v.17), demonstrated by examples (vv.18-25), and restated with finality (v.26).

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Let not many of you become teachers, my brothers, knowing that we shall receive a greater judgment.
verses.note: GREEK: "διδάσκαλοι" (didaskaloi, Strong's G1320) denotes teachers or instructors, a role of high authority in the early assembly. "κρίμα" (krima, G2917) here means judgment or verdict, not merely criticism — teachers face stricter divine evaluation because their words shape the faith of others. THEOLOGICAL: James writes as one who includes himself ("we shall receive"), indicating apostolic humility before the judgment seat of Christ (2 Cor 5:10).

verses.flow: For in many things we all stumble. If anyone does not stumble in word, this one is a perfect man, able to bridle also the whole body.
verses.note: GREEK: "πταίομεν" (ptaiomen, G4417) means to stumble or trip, used metaphorically for moral failure. "χαλιναγωγῆσαι" (chalinagōgēsai, G5468) means to lead with a bridle, a vivid compound word (chalinos + agō) unique to James in the NT (also Jas 1:26). "τέλειος" (teleios, G5046) signifies completeness or maturity, not sinless perfection.

verses.flow: Now if we put the bits into the mouths of horses for them to obey us, we turn about also their whole body.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural neuter
verb present passive participle nominative plural neuter
verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: Behold, also the ships, being so great and being driven by harsh winds, are turned about by a very small rudder, wherever the impulse of the one steering wills.
So also the tongue is a small member and boasts great things. Behold, how small a fire kindles how great a forest!

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine
verb present active participle nominative singular feminine
verb present passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And the tongue is a fire, the world of unrighteousness. The tongue is set among our members, the one defiling the whole body and setting on fire the wheel of birth, and being set on fire by Gehenna.
verses.note: GREEK: "τὸν τροχὸν τῆς γενέσεως" (ton trochon tēs geneseōs) is a difficult phrase, literally "the wheel of origin/birth." Some see Orphic or Stoic background, but in context it likely means the entire course of human existence from birth onward. "γεέννης" (geennēs, G1067) refers to Gehenna, the Valley of Hinnom (Ge-Hinnom), associated with divine judgment and destructive fire (Matt 5:22; Mark 9:43). THEOLOGICAL: The tongue's destructive power is portrayed as having its ultimate source in Gehenna itself — demonic instigation behind human speech that destroys.

verses.flow: For every kind of beasts and of birds, of creeping things and of things in the sea, is tamed and has been tamed by the human kind;
but the tongue no one of men is able to tame — an unstable evil, full of death-bearing poison.
verses.note: GREEK: "ἀκατάστατον" (akatastaton, G182) means unstable or restless, the same root as "ἀκαταστασία" (disorder) in v.16. "θανατηφόρου" (thanatēphorou, G2287) is a NT hapax legomenon, a compound of "thanatos" (death) + "pherō" (to bear), meaning death-carrying or death-bearing. The imagery evokes the deadly serpent, recalling Genesis 3 and the deception wrought by the tongue of the ancient serpent.

words.m: verb perfect active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: With it we bless the Lord and Father, and with it we curse men, those having been made according to the likeness of God.
verses.note: GREEK: "κύριον" (kyrion, G2962) here refers to the Lord, likely God the Father in view of "and Father" following. "ὁμοίωσιν" (homoiōsin, G3669) echoes the LXX of Genesis 1:26 ("κατὰ ὁμοίωσιν"), where humanity is made according to the likeness of God. TYPOLOGY: James grounds the dignity of every human being in the imago Dei from creation — to curse a person made in God's likeness is to assault the image of the Creator Himself. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "likeness of God" language connects humanity to the divine realm; humans bear the image of the Most High, set as His representatives on earth (Ps 8:5-6; Gen 1:26-27).

verses.flow: Out of the same mouth comes forth blessing and cursing. It ought not, my brothers, that these things should be so.
Does the spring send forth out of the same opening the sweet and the bitter?
Is a fig tree able, my brothers, to produce olives, or a vine figs? Neither can salt water produce sweet.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The fig tree imagery recalls Jesus's cursing of the barren fig tree (Matt 21:19; Mark 11:13-14), a symbol of Israel's fruitlessness. James argues from nature that a single source cannot produce contradictory fruit — a principle rooted in Jesus's own teaching (Matt 7:16-18). The salt and sweet water illustration may evoke the bitter waters of Marah made sweet by God (Exod 15:23-25).

verses.flow: Who is wise and understanding among you? Let him show out of the good conduct his works in meekness of wisdom.
But if you have bitter jealousy and selfish ambition in your heart, do not boast and lie against the truth.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: This is not the wisdom coming down from above, but is earthly, natural, demonic.
verses.note: GREEK: "ἐπίγειος" (epigeios, G1919) means earthly, upon the earth — contrasted with "from above" (ἄνωθεν). "ψυχική" (psychikē, G5591) derives from "psychē" (soul) and denotes the merely natural or soulish, that which operates apart from the Spirit (cf. 1 Cor 2:14; Jude 19). "δαιμονιώδης" (daimoniōdēs, G1141) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning demon-like or demonic in character. THEOLOGICAL: James presents a descending triad — earthly, natural, demonic — showing that wisdom not from God degenerates toward the realm of hostile spiritual powers.

verses.flow: For where jealousy and selfish ambition are, there is disorder and every worthless deed.
But the wisdom from above is first indeed pure, then peaceable, gentle, easily entreated, full of mercy and of good fruits, without partiality, without hypocrisy.
verses.note: GREEK: "εὐπειθής" (eupeithēs, G2138) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning easily persuaded or compliant, from "eu" (well) + "peithō" (to persuade). "ἀδιάκριτος" (adiakritos, G87) is also a NT hapax, meaning without partiality or without wavering. THEOLOGICAL: The seven attributes following "pure" may echo the sevenfold Spirit of God (Isa 11:2). This heavenly wisdom is the character of God Himself manifest in believers — the same wisdom personified in Proverbs 8 and ultimately embodied in Christ (1 Cor 1:30; Col 2:3).

words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: And the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace by those making peace.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse forms the conclusion to the entire discourse on wisdom. "Fruit of righteousness" echoes Proverbs 11:30 and Philippians 1:11. The passive "is sown" suggests divine agency — God produces righteousness through those who cultivate peace. TYPOLOGY: The peacemakers here correspond to those Jesus declared blessed (Matt 5:9), who are called "sons of God" — those whose character reflects the heavenly wisdom described in v.17.

words.m: verb present middle participle genitive plural feminine

verses.flow: From whence come wars and from whence come fightings among you? Is it not from hence, out of your pleasures that wage war in your members?
verses.note: GREEK: "Wars" (πόλεμοι, polemoi, Strong's G4171) denotes large-scale conflicts, while "fightings" (μάχαι, machai, G3163) indicates quarrels or disputes. "Pleasures" (ἡδονῶν, hēdonōn, G2237) is the root of English "hedonism," here personified as soldiers waging campaign-style war (στρατευομένων, strateuomenōn) within one's own members. James echoes Paul's inner-war language (Rom 7:23).

verses.flow: You desire and do not have; you murder and are jealous and are not able to obtain; you fight and wage war. You do not have because you do not ask.
You ask and do not receive, because you ask wrongly, that you may spend it upon your pleasures.
Adulteresses! Do you not know that the friendship of the world is enmity with God? Whoever therefore purposes to be a friend of the world makes himself an enemy of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Adulteresses" (μοιχαλίδες, moichalides) employs the OT prophetic metaphor of spiritual adultery — Israel's unfaithfulness to YHWH as covenant-spouse (Hos 2:2; Jer 3:6-10; Ezek 16). James applies this covenantal imagery directly to the messianic community. Friendship (φιλία, philia) with the world-system constitutes a rival allegiance incompatible with loyalty to God, placing the worldly believer in the position of covenant-breaker.

verses.flow: Or do you suppose that the Scripture speaks in vain? The Spirit which He caused to dwell in us yearns with jealousy.
verses.note: GREEK: This verse is notoriously difficult to interpret. "The Spirit which He caused to dwell" (τὸ πνεῦμα ὃ κατῴκισεν, to pneuma ho katōkisen) may refer to the Holy Spirit whom God placed in believers, or the human spirit God implanted. "Yearns with jealousy" (πρὸς φθόνον ἐπιποθεῖ, pros phthonon epipothei) — ἐπιποθέω (G1971) means to long for intensely. The exact OT quotation James references remains unidentified, possibly a free rendering of Exod 20:5 or Deut 32:10-12. DIVINE_COUNCIL: God's jealousy for His people reflects His exclusive covenantal claim over those in whom His Spirit dwells.

verses.flow: But He gives greater grace; therefore it says, "God opposes the proud, but gives grace to the humble."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Prov 3:34 (LXX), also cited in 1 Pet 5:5. The pattern of divine resistance to the proud and favor toward the humble is a consistent scriptural principle (Ps 138:6; Isa 57:15; Luke 1:52). "Greater grace" (μείζονα χάριν) indicates that God's gracious provision exceeds the jealous longing of verse 5 — grace triumphs over the downward pull of worldly desire.

verses.flow: Submit yourselves therefore to God. But resist the devil, and he will flee from you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The imperative "submit" (ὑποτάγητε, hypotagēte, G5293, aorist passive) denotes placing oneself under God's authority — a military term for ranking under a commander. "Resist" (ἀντίστητε, antistēte, G436, aorist active) the devil (τῷ διαβόλῳ, tō diabolō, "the slanderer") carries the promise of his flight. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The devil is presented as a hostile spiritual being whose power is limited; he must flee when confronted by a believer aligned under God's sovereign authority (cf. Eph 6:11-13; 1 Pet 5:8-9).

verses.flow: Draw near to God, and He will draw near to you. Cleanse your hands, sinners, and purify your hearts, double-minded.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Cleanse your hands...purify your hearts" echoes priestly purification language (Exod 30:19-21; Lev 16:30) and Ps 24:3-4 — "clean hands and a pure heart" as the requirement for ascending to the Lord's holy place. "Double-minded" (δίψυχοι, dipsychoi, G1374) is a word found only in James in the NT (also Jas 1:8), describing the divided loyalty condemned in verse 4.

verses.flow: Be afflicted and mourn and weep. Let your laughter be turned to mourning, and your joy to dejection.
Humble yourselves before the Lord, and He will exalt you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Humble yourselves before the Lord" (ταπεινώθητε ἐνώπιον κυρίου) echoes the consistent biblical pattern that self-abasement before God precedes divine exaltation (1 Pet 5:6; Matt 23:12; Luke 14:11; Luke 18:14). The passive-voice promise "He will exalt you" (ὑψώσει ὑμᾶς) places the timing and manner entirely in God's sovereign hands.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Do not speak against one another, brothers. The one who speaks against a brother or judges his brother speaks against the law and judges the law. But if you judge the law, you are not a doer of the law but a judge.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: There is one Lawgiver and Judge, the One who is able to save and to destroy. But who are you, the one who judges the neighbor?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "One Lawgiver and Judge" (εἷς νομοθέτης καὶ κριτής) — νομοθέτης (nomothetēs, G3550) is a NT hapax legomenon, appearing only here. James affirms the singular divine prerogative over both law-giving and judgment, the power "to save and to destroy" (σῶσαι καὶ ἀπολέσαι). This echoes Deut 32:39 and Isa 33:22, consolidating legislative and judicial authority in God alone, and thus rebuking presumptuous human judgment of one's neighbor.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Come now, you who say, "Today or tomorrow we will go into this city and spend a year there and trade and gain profit" —

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular feminine
verb present passive participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: you who do not know what is of the morrow — for what is your life? For you are a vapor that appears for a little while and then vanishes.
verses.note: GREEK: "Vapor" (ἀτμίς, atmis, G822) occurs only here and in Acts 2:19 in the NT. The imagery of human transience as mist or breath echoes Ps 39:5-6, Ps 144:4, and Eccl 6:12. "Appears for a little while and then vanishes" (πρὸς ὀλίγον φαινομένη ἔπειτα καὶ ἀφανιζομένη) — the participles frame life as a brief manifestation that dissolves, underscoring radical dependence on God's sovereign will (v.15).

verses.flow: Instead of your saying, "If the Lord wills, we shall both live and do this or that."
But now you boast in your arrogance. All such boasting is evil.

words.m: verb perfect active participle dative singular masculine
verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: Therefore, to the one who knows to do good and does not do it, to him it is sin.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse functions as a concluding aphorism with universal application beyond its immediate context. It defines sin not merely as active transgression but as omission — knowing the good (καλόν, kalon) and failing to do it. This "sin of omission" principle has broad ethical implications and connects to Jesus's parable teachings (Luke 12:47-48). The dative αὐτῷ ("to him") makes the judgment intensely personal.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle dative plural feminine

verses.flow: Come now, you rich ones, weep, howling over your miseries that are coming upon you.
Your wealth has rotted, and your garments have become moth-eaten.
Your gold and your silver have corroded, and their corrosion shall be for a testimony against you, and shall eat your flesh as fire. You have stored up treasure in the last days.
verses.note: GREEK: κατίωται (katioōtai, from katióō) means "has become corroded/rusted" — a perfect passive indicating an existing state of decay. The verb is rare in biblical Greek. THEOLOGICAL: The irony is devastating: they stored up treasure (ἐθησαυρίσατε, ethēsaurisate) but what they stored is evidence for their own judgment "in the last days" (ἐν ἐσχάταις ἡμέραις), echoing Jesus' teaching on true treasure (Matt 6:19-20).

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive plural masculine
verb perfect passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: Behold, the wages of the laborers who reaped your fields, which has been withheld by you, cries out, and the cries of those who reaped have entered into the ears of the Lord of Sabaoth.
verses.note: GREEK: Κυρίου Σαβαώθ (Kyriou Sabaōth) — "Lord of Sabaoth" (i.e., Lord of Hosts/Armies), a direct transliteration from Hebrew צְבָאוֹת (tsevaʾot), preserved from the LXX of Isaiah 5:9. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This title evokes God as the commander of the heavenly armies, the cosmic sovereign who hears the oppressed. James deliberately invokes the most majestic OT divine-council title to heighten the terror of judgment against exploitative rich. TYPOLOGY: Echoes Deuteronomy 24:14-15 and Leviticus 19:13 — withholding wages was covenant violation crying to God.

verses.flow: You lived in luxury upon the earth and lived in self-indulgence; you fattened your hearts in a day of slaughter.
You condemned, you murdered the righteous one; he does not resist you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: τὸν δίκαιον (ton dikaion, "the righteous one") is singular, possibly referring both to the generic righteous poor person and typologically to Christ Himself — the Righteous One (Acts 3:14, 7:52) who did not resist His murderers (Isa 53:7). GREEK: οὐκ ἀντιτάσσεται (ouk antitassetai) — present middle: "he does not set himself against you," evoking the non-resistance of the innocent.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Be patient, therefore, brothers, until the coming of the Lord. Behold, the farmer awaits the precious fruit of the earth, being patient over it until it receives the early and the latter rain.
Be patient, you also; establish your hearts, for the coming of the Lord has drawn near.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: ἡ παρουσία τοῦ κυρίου ἤγγικεν (hē parousia tou kyriou ēngiken) — "the coming of the Lord has drawn near." The perfect tense ἤγγικεν indicates a completed approach with ongoing relevance — the Lord's return is perpetually imminent. This is the same language used by Jesus in Mark 1:15 regarding the kingdom. GREEK: στηρίξατε (stērixate, aorist imperative of stērizō, Strong's G4741) — "establish firmly," used of resolute, unwavering commitment.

verses.flow: Do not groan against one another, brothers, that you may not be judged. Behold, the Judge stands before the doors.
Take as an example, brothers, of suffering and of patience, the prophets who spoke in the name of the Lord.

words.m: verb aorist active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: Behold, we count blessed those who endured. You have heard of the endurance of Job, and the purpose of the Lord you have seen, that the Lord is full of compassion and merciful.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Job is cited as the supreme OT example of endurance under suffering without understanding. τὸ τέλος κυρίου (to telos kyriou) — "the purpose/outcome of the Lord" — refers to how God brought Job's trial to a redemptive conclusion (Job 42:10-17). GREEK: πολύσπλαγχνος (polysplanchnos, Strong's G4184) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "very compassionate" or "full of tender mercy," compounding πολύς (much) + σπλάγχνον (inward parts/bowels of compassion). This rare word emphasizes the superabundance of divine mercy.

verses.flow: But before all things, my brothers, do not swear, neither by the heaven, nor by the earth, nor any other oath; but let your Yes be yes, and your No, no, that you may not fall under judgment.
Does anyone among you suffer? Let him pray. Is anyone cheerful? Let him sing psalms.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Is anyone among you sick? Let him call for the elders of the assembly, and let them pray over him, having anointed him with oil in the name of the Lord.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Anointing with oil (ἐλαίῳ, elaiō) in the ancient world served both medicinal (Luke 10:34) and sacramental purposes. The combination of physical care and prayer reflects the holistic Jewish understanding of healing. GREEK: πρεσβυτέρους τῆς ἐκκλησίας (presbyterous tēs ekklēsias) — "elders of the assembly" — indicates established leadership structures in the early Jewish-Christian communities. The term ekklēsia here carries its Septuagintal sense of the gathered covenant people.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine
verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And the prayer of faith shall save the one who is weary, and the Lord shall raise him up; and if he has committed sins, it shall be forgiven him.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: Therefore, confess your sins to one another, and pray for one another, so that you may be healed. The effective prayer of a righteous one avails much.
verses.note: GREEK: ἐνεργουμένη (energoumenē) is a present middle/passive participle of ἐνεργέω (energeō, Strong's G1754). If middle: "the prayer of a righteous one, being effectually at work, avails much." If passive: "being empowered/energized [by the Spirit]." The ambiguity may be intentional — righteous prayer is both humanly earnest and divinely empowered. THEOLOGICAL: The linkage of confession, intercession, and healing reflects the covenant community as a place of mutual restoration.

verses.flow: Elijah was a man of like nature with us, and he prayed with prayer that it would not rain, and it did not rain upon the earth for three years and six months.
verses.note: GREEK: προσευχῇ προσηύξατο (proseuchē prosēuxato) — a Hebraism using the cognate dative construction (infinitive absolute pattern), literally "he prayed with prayer," meaning "he prayed earnestly/fervently." TYPOLOGY: Elijah as a man ὁμοιοπαθής (homoiopathēs, "of like nature/suffering") demystifies prophetic power — extraordinary results come not from extraordinary persons but from extraordinary faith directed toward a faithful God (1 Kings 17:1; 18:42-45).

verses.flow: And again he prayed, and the heaven gave rain, and the earth brought forth its fruit.
My brothers, if anyone among you wanders from the truth, and someone turns him back,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: let him know that the one who turns back a sinner from the error of his way shall save his soul from death and shall cover a multitude of sins.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: καλύψει πλῆθος ἁμαρτιῶν (kalypsei plēthos hamartiōn) — "shall cover a multitude of sins" — echoes Proverbs 10:12 (LXX) and 1 Peter 4:8. The covering of sins through restorative love within the community reflects the atoning work of Christ applied in the fellowship of believers. GREEK: ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ (psychēn autou) — "his soul" — the antecedent of αὐτοῦ is debated: it may refer to the soul of the sinner being rescued, or to the soul of the one who restores him. The former reading fits the immediate context of saving from spiritual death.
